title: Electric air taxis are about to take flight in 26 states
text: Electric air taxis are about to take flight in 26 states. Several companies developing electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft have received regulatory approvals and are preparing for commercial operations across the United States. The Federal Aviation Administration has been working closely with manufacturers to establish safety standards for this new mode of transportation. Companies like Joby Aviation, Archer Aviation, and Lilium are at the forefront of this revolution, each developing unique aircraft designs that promise to transform urban mobility. The aircraft are designed to carry passengers short distances, typically between city centers and airports, offering a faster alternative to ground transportation. Industry experts predict that the first commercial flights could begin as early as next year, with fares initially comparable to premium ride-sharing services. The technology relies on distributed electric propulsion systems that are quieter and more environmentally friendly than traditional helicopters. Several major cities have already begun planning vertiport infrastructure to support the new air taxi networks. Safety remains the top priority, with each aircraft undergoing thousands of hours of testing before receiving certification. The potential market for urban air mobility is estimated to be worth billions of dollars by 2030.

title: BBC Technology News Article
text: Technology continues to reshape how we live and work, with artificial intelligence leading the latest wave of innovation. Recent developments in machine learning have produced systems capable of understanding and generating human language with remarkable accuracy. These advances are being applied across industries, from healthcare diagnostics to financial analysis, creating new opportunities and challenges alike. Researchers are also making progress in quantum computing, which promises to solve problems that are currently intractable for classical computers. The convergence of AI and quantum computing could accelerate scientific discovery in areas such as drug development and materials science. Meanwhile, cybersecurity experts warn that the same technologies that offer tremendous benefits also create new vulnerabilities that must be addressed. Governments around the world are grappling with how to regulate these powerful technologies while still encouraging innovation. The debate over data privacy continues to intensify as more personal information is collected and processed by AI systems. Consumer technology companies are responding by building more transparency and control into their products. The pace of change shows no signs of slowing, with breakthroughs in robotics, biotechnology, and space exploration all contributing to a rapidly evolving technological landscape.

title: Building a Text-to-Speech Engine in Pure C
text: Building a text-to-speech engine from scratch in pure C is an ambitious but incredibly educational project. The process involves several key components: text normalization, phoneme conversion, prosody generation, and audio synthesis. First, the input text must be normalized by expanding abbreviations, numbers, and special characters into their spoken equivalents. Next, the normalized text is converted into phonemes using a pronunciation dictionary or rule-based system. The phoneme sequence is then used to generate prosody parameters such as pitch, duration, and energy for each sound. Finally, the audio waveform is synthesized using techniques like concatenative synthesis or formant synthesis. In this implementation, we chose formant synthesis for its simplicity and small memory footprint. The engine uses a set of digital filters to model the human vocal tract, with parameters that can be adjusted to produce different vowel and consonant sounds. One of the biggest challenges was handling coarticulation effects, where the pronunciation of one phoneme is influenced by its neighbors. We addressed this by implementing smooth interpolation between adjacent phoneme targets. The resulting engine produces intelligible speech with a distinctive robotic quality, running entirely without external dependencies.
